text:
Abstract — Community detection in complex networks is a widely studied topic, with both theoretical and practical implications. Despite a large body of research that has been devoted to non-overlapping communities, there are many scenarios in which communities may be allowed to overlap. Overlapping community detection, or multi-community membership, is thus of increasing interest and the aim of this paper is to provide a detailed review of work in this field to date, reviewing algorithms, evaluation criteria and tools for benchmarking graph clustering algorithms in this context. Within this framework we conduct an in-depth comparison of 14 graph clustering algorithms to consider their performance under the different criteria defined above. In addition to the more common criteria such as modularity, conductance and clustering coefficient for evaluating non-overlapping community structures, we propose new criteria to estimate the accuracy of algorithms in identifying the number of overlapping nodes, which may reflect the amount of over- and under-detection of such nodes.

We analyze the performance of SLPA, OSLOM, Game, and COPRA on community detection using community-level metrics like NMIs and the Omega Index as well as node-level metrics like F-score. Our analysis reveals many interesting results. First of all, SLPA, OSLOM, Game and COPRA are mostly the winners in low overlapping density (asd) graphs. Second, SLPA and Game are the more stable algorithms in high overlapping density (asd) graphs. Finally, although all algorithms perform very well, there is a clear picture across different versions of all real world social networks we have access to: in almost all cases the fraction of overlapping nodes in the graph is less than 30% and most overlapping nodes are in exactly 2 or 3 communities.
